coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of N doped-CQDs/Ni doped-ZnO nanocomposites for visible light photodegradation of organic pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Behnood, Reza
Sodeifian, Gholamhossein
coredata.dc:description: Nowadays, visible light-driven nanophotocatalysts are being developed for high-efficiency removal of organic pollutants. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely investigated as a superior candidate for improving the photocatalytic activity of various nanocomposites. In this paper, a nickel-doped ZnO photocatalyst was surface-modified using hydrothermally prepared nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for the first time. The synthesized nanocomposite (N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO) was then characterized using SEM, EDX, XRD, FT-IR, PL, and DRS analyses. The presence of Ni and N-CQDs and their effects on the band gap reduction of the ZnO and improvement of electron/hole pairs separation were confirmed with XRD, EDX, PL and DRS tests. The photocatalytic activity of the N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO was tested for methylene blue dye removal from aqueous solution under visible light irradiation. After 120 min of irradiation, the optimum photocatalyst could degrade about 87 % of the MB under the visible light irradiation. The kinetics of the MB dye degradation was modeled according to Langmuir–Hinshelwood model and k was 0.015. Also, the effects of pH and photocatalyst dosage were examined for optimizing the photocatalytic activity of the N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO. According to the results, the optimum pH was approximated at 11 (alkaline) and the best results were obtained with 0.05 g of the photocatalyst per 50 ml of a 50 ppm MB dye solution for the top synthesized nanophotocatalyst (1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO). The reusability experiments for the prepared nanophotocatalyst showed that the prepared N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO possessed excellent stability after four reaction cycles. The findings of this study can be used in industrial applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Synthesis
N-CQDs
N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO nanocomposites
Photocatalyst
Visible light
Methylene blue

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Preparation of Ni-ZnO modified with N-CQDs (N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO)
Effect of nickel dopant on photoactivity of ZnO
Effect of N-CQDs on photoactivity of 6wt.% Ni-ZnO
Effect of pH on photoactivity of 1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO
Effect of dosage of 1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO nanophotocatalyst
Kinetic study of the MB degradation
Mechanism of photocatalytic degradation using N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank research deputy of University of Kashan for providing financial support for this project (Grant # Pajoohaneh-1398/16 ). Moreover, the authors express their thanks to the advanced materials and nanotechnology laboratory of University of Kashan for providing experimental facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of prepared ZnO (a, b), Ni-ZnO (c, d) and N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO (e, f) with different amount of nickel dopant.
SEM images of ZnO (a), Ni-ZnO (b) and N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO (c) with different magnification.
TEM images of N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO with two different magnifications.
(a)EDX spectrum of Ni-ZnO. (b)-f. EDX elemental mapping of Ni-ZnO.
(a) EDX spectrum of N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO. (b)-h. EDX elemental mapping of N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO.

FT-IR spectra for ZnO (blue line), N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO (green line) and N-CQDs (red line) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of ZnO, 6 wt.% Ni-ZnO and 1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO. The inset shows
α η ν
2
vs.
h ν
plot of prepared samples.
PL Spectra of 6 wt.% Ni-ZnO and 1.5 N-CQDs/6% Ni-ZnO.
Visible light photo-degradation of MB over Ni-ZnO photocatalyst with different amount of nickel dopant (a) and degradation efficiency of MB over different Ni-ZnO photocatalyst (b).
Visible light photo-degradation of MB over N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO photocatalyst with different amount of N-CQDs. The inset shows degradation efficiency of MB over different N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO photocatalyst.
Photocatalytic degradation of MB over N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO photocatalyst in different pH of dye solution.
Photocatalytic degradation of MB over different loaded of N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO photocatalyst.
Data modeling according to Langmuir-Hinshelwood model for Ni-ZnO and N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO photocatalysts.
Reusability experiments for degradation of MB over N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO photocatalyst.
EDX analysis of N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO photocatalyst after photodegradation process.
Mechanism of MB degradation over N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO photocatalyst.
Lists works that synthesized ZnO and CQDs nanocomposite.

row.entry: D-fructose, NaOH, zinc acetate, terephthalic acid
zinc acetate dehydrate, ammonia solution, carbon black pigment, H2SO4, HNO3,
modified Hummer’s method for CQDs, physical mixing for CQD/N-ZnO
solvothermal process for GQDs and electrochemical deposition process for films
citric acid, diethylene glycol, zinc stearate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Line formula and parameters for modeling of MB degradation data.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of N doped-CQDs/Ni doped-ZnO nanocomposites for visible light photodegradation of organic pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, University of Kashan, 87317-53153, Kashan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Advanced Materials and Nanotechnology, University of Kashan, 87317-53153, Kashan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Advanced Materials and Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Modeling and Simulation Centre, Faculty of Engineering, University of Kashan, 87317-53153, Kashan, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, University of Kashan, 87317-53153, Kashan, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nowadays, visible light-driven nanophotocatalysts are being developed for high-efficiency removal of organic pollutants. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely investigated as a superior candidate for improving the photocatalytic activity of various nanocomposites. In this paper, a nickel-doped ZnO photocatalyst was surface-modified using hydrothermally prepared nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for the first time. The synthesized nanocomposite (N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO) was then characterized using SEM, EDX, XRD, FT-IR, PL, and DRS analyses. The presence of Ni and N-CQDs and their effects on the band gap reduction of the ZnO and improvement of electron/hole pairs separation were confirmed with XRD, EDX, PL and DRS tests. The photocatalytic activity of the N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO was tested for methylene blue dye removal from aqueous solution under visible light irradiation. After 120 min of irradiation, the optimum photocatalyst could degrade about 87 % of the MB under the visible light irradiation. The kinetics of the MB dye degradation was modeled according to Langmuir–Hinshelwood model and k was 0.015. Also, the effects of pH and photocatalyst dosage were examined for optimizing the photocatalytic activity of the N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO. According to the results, the optimum pH was approximated at 11 (alkaline) and the best results were obtained with 0.05 g of the photocatalyst per 50 ml of a 50 ppm MB dye solution for the top synthesized nanophotocatalyst (1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO). The reusability experiments for the prepared nanophotocatalyst showed that the prepared N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO possessed excellent stability after four reaction cycles. The findings of this study can be used in industrial applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new class of carbon nanomaterials, have drawn great deals of attention in numerous technological applications. The CQDs are described by spherical morphology of sub-10 nm carbon nanoparticles [1]. Owing to their nontoxicity character and excellent electrical and optical properties, CQDs have been widely studied as a superior candidate for photo-induced applications, such as nanocomposite photocatalysts, solar cells, and biological imaging [2].

Recently, to overcome the challenges associated with common semiconductors in photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants, CQDs have been considered for preparing superior photocatalysts in combination with semiconductors such as TiO2, ZnO, etc. In principle, the photocatalytic process is a photo-induced reaction; hence the photocatalytic efficiency is mainly dependent on the ability of the photocatalyst to absorb the light [2,3].

The main limitation suffered by the conventional semiconductors is their wide optical band gap, implying that they are merely able to absorb shorter wavelengths out of the UV-range sunlight. Another drawback of the common semiconductors in the photocatalytic applications is related to the fast recombination of the photo-generated electrons and holes, which can effectively reduce the quantum efficiency of the semiconductor to form highly reactive oxygen and hydroxyl radicals (•O2ˉ, •OHˉ, etc.) [2,3].

High electron conductivity of the CQDs makes them appropriate candidates for improving the photocatalytic properties of TiO2 and ZnO photocatalysts. In addition, surface functional groups of the CQDs can promote the adsorption of the dye molecules on the surface of the photocatalyst particles [4].

During the recent past, new developments have been reported on the application of CQDs in photocatalysts. In this regard, Rahbar et al. prepared S, N-doped CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite as an efficient photocatalyst for visible light-degradation of the so-called acid red 88 [4]. Zhang et al. reported the application of CQDs/TiO2/Fe2O3 composite for degradation of the methylene blue (MB) under visible light irradiation [5]. Bai et al. synthesized the CQDs-modified N-TiO2-x nanocomposite and reported its photocatalytic capability for photo-reduction of Cr (VI) under visible light irradiation [6]. Zhang et al. successfully employed the coal tar pitch as natural CQD for modification of TiO2 as an effective photocatalyst for degradation of rhodamine B under visible light irradiation by a simple and environmentally friendly one-step solvothermal method [7].

Zinc oxide (ZnO) has been considered as an alternative to the TiO2 photocatalyst, offering great potentials for photocatalytic application at large scales because of its lower cost of production compared to the TiO2 [8]. However, similar to the TiO2 photocatalyst, the ZnO exhibits a wide optical band gap (Eg = 3.4 eV3), making it inefficient under visible light irradiation. Many strategies have been proposed for visible-light sensitization of ZnO, including the use of doping cations (Cu [9], Ni [10], Fe [11], etc.) and anions (N [12], S [13], F [14], etc.) and coupling with other semiconductors (i.e. CuO [15], Fe2O3 [16], TiO2 [17], etc.).

Due to their sufficient electron transportation ability, the carbon nanomaterials have been seen as promising candidates for promoting the separation of photo-generated charge carriers and hence improving the photocatalytic efficiency of semiconductors such as ZnO. Therefore, carbon-containing nanocomposite semiconductors (e.g. carbon nanotubes, GQDs, fullerenes, etc.) have been greatly evaluated for photocatalytic applications. For this purpose, Samdi et al. prepared a visible-light photocatalyst using a combination of multi-wall carbon nanotubes (MWCNT)/ZnO [18]. Xu et al. produced a hybrid of graphene/ZnO photocatalyst for the degradation of the MB [19]. Sodeifian et al. modified the ZnO and CuO, as nanophotocatalysts, with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), with their results demonstrating that the N-GQDs could significantly enhance the photoactivity of the ZnO while imposing no significant effect on the CuO [20]. Table 1 lists some of the other studies where the focus has been on the synthesis of nanocomposites of ZnO with CQDs or GQDs for different purposes [21–25].

To the best of our knowledge, there is not any report on the synthesis of Ni-doped ZnO (Ni-ZnO) nanoparticles modified with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). Therefore, the N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO nanocomposite was synthesized, for the first time, and examined for the degradation of MB dye under visible light irradiation. N-CQDs solution was prepared using L-ascorbic acid (as carbon source) and urea (as nitrogen source) through a hydrothermal reaction. A simple and straight precipitation route was employed for preparing the Ni-ZnO. Finally, the optimum photocatalyst was evaluated under various conditions for maximal degradation yield.

All chemicals were purchased from Merck and used without any further purification. These included zinc acetate (Zn(CH3COO)2.2H2O), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, polyethylene glycol PEG, Mw = 10,000, nickel nitrate NiNO3)2.6H2O), L-ascorbic acid, and urea.

Firstly, 1 g of PEG was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized (DI) water. Next, 1 mmol of Zn(CH3COO)2.2H2O was dissolved in 20 mL of DI water (solution I), 1.5 mmol of Na2CO3 was dissolved in 20 mL of DI water (solution II), and appropriate amount of Ni(NO3)2.6H2O was dissolved in 20 mL of DI water (solution III). Under stirring, the solutions I through III were added, simultaneously and in a drop-wise fashion, to the PEG solution to form a capping and size-controlling agent. After 2 h of stirring to complete the reaction, the solid was separated and dried at 120 °C for 10 h. Finally, the dried composite was heated at 500 °C for 2 h to form a crystalline product with no impurities. This synthesis route was followed for different dosages of the Ni dopant (0, 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 wt.%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prepare the N-CQDs solution, 2 mmol of l-ascorbic acid and 6 mmol of urea were dissolved in 25 mL of DI water. The prepared solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept there at 160 °C for 8 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Ni-ZnO modified with N-CQDs (N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO)
ce:section.ce:para: In order to modify the Ni-ZnO nanoparticles by N-CQDs, firstly, 0.2 g of as-prepared Ni-ZnO nanoparticles was dispersed into 50 mL of DI water under vigorous stirring. Then certain amount of prepared N-CQDs solution was added to the mixture followed by continuous stirring for 2 h. After that, the solid product was separated using centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 5 min and twice-washed with DI water and ethanol before being dried in an oven at 70 °C overnight. This procedure was carried out for different amounts of the N-CQDs solution (0, 1, 1.5, 2 and 2.5 mL).

Surface morphology of the prepared samples were studied using field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi S4160) at a resolution of about 5 nm and transmission electron microscope (TEM, Zeiss-EM10C-100 KV). Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) was performed on a Shimadzu Varian 4300 spectrometer to determine the surface chemical groups. Crystallinity of the prepared Ni-ZnO samples was investigated by X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) analysis using a Philips X’pert Pro MPD under Cu-kα irradiation at room temperature in the 2θ range of 10°-80°. Optical properties of the prepared samples were analyzed by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS using A V-670 UV–vis-NIR). The FE-SEM apparatus was equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscope for indicating the elemental composition of the nanocomposites. The EDX tests were carried out at an accelerated voltage of 20 kV. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples were obtained by a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer at excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

A centrifuge (Hettich, EBA 200, Germany) was used to separate the nanoparticles from different solutions. Dye concentration assessment was performed on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Unico, SQ-4802, double beam, USA).

The photocatalytic experiments were conducted by prepared N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO samples for photodegradation of MB dye in aqueous medium under visible light irradiation. An Osram lamp (HQIBT 400 W/D/DE40FLH1) at a distance of 25 cm to the reaction solution was used as the visible light source in the photocatalyst box (a 60 × 60 × 60 cm wooden box with its inner surfaces covered with mirrors).

The photocatalytic reaction was carried out by dispersing 50 mg of the prepared sample into 50 mL of MB solution (50 ppm). First, the suspension was stirred in dark for 30 min to achieve the adsorption/desorption equilibrium between the dye molecules and the photocatalyst particles. Then, the photocatalytic reaction was triggered by exposing the reaction solution to the visible light irradiation. At certain time intervals (every 20 min), 4 mL of the dye solution was pipetted and then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 4 min to separate the photocatalyst particles. The concentration of the dye solution was calculated using the absorbance data from the UV–vis spectrometer at 665 nm, as follows:

The MB dye concentration (%) after t minutes:
(1)
C o n c e n t r a t i o n =
A t
A 0
×
C 0
× 100
%

The photodegradation rate was then computed via the following formula:
(2)
D e g r a d a t i o n
% =
C 0
− C
C 0
× 100
% =
A 0
−
A t
A 0
× 100
%
where
C 0
is the initial concentration, C denotes the concentration at time t, and
A 0
and
A t
are the initial MB absorbance and the absorbance after t minutes of irradiation, respectively.

Fig. 1 presents the crystallinity of the prepared samples as obtained based on the XRD analysis, displaying diffraction patterns at 32.20°, 34.83°, 36.63°, 47.94°, 56.92°, 63.20°, 66.85°, 68.33°, 69.49°, 72.90° and 77.33°, which are clearly corresponding to the diffraction patterns of hexagonal ZnO (JCPDS No. 005-0664). For the sample prepared with Ni at 2 wt.%, no diffraction peak was observed for the nickel oxide, revealing the successful introducing of the nickel atoms into the hexagonal structure of the ZnO. However, with increasing the content of nickel, an additional diffraction peak appeared at 2θ = 43.61°, which was assigned to the (220) diffraction plane of NiO (JCPDS No. 001-1239). It was also observed that further increase in the amount of nickel led to some phase separation and formation of binary oxide as NiO/ZnO [26–30]. Because of the high dispersion of the CQD particles on the surface of the Ni-ZnO and the negligible amount of CQDs in comparison with the Ni-ZnO, no diffraction peak was evident for the prepared N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO nanocomposite [4,31].

Fig. 2 presents the FESEM images of the samples, namely ZnO (a, b), Ni-ZnO (c, d) and N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO (e, f), at different magnifications to evaluate their morphology and internal structure. The figure reveals spherical morphology of the prepared samples with an average particle size of 25 nm, and that the introducing of nickel dopant into the the ZnO structure and surface modification of the Ni-ZnO nanoparticles with N-CQDs had no remarkable impact on the morphology of the ZnO nanoparticles. Deeper insights into the morphology of Ni-ZnO and N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO particles were provided by TEM experiments (Fig. 3 a and b for N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO nanocomposites at two magnifications). Fig. 3b clearly expresses the uniform distribution of the N-CQDs with a near spherical morphology and particle sizes smaller than 10 nm, as indicated by arrows.

Fig. 4 a displays EDX spectrum of the Ni-ZnO samples. Elemental microanalysis of the Ni-ZnO nanocomposite indicated that the relative amounts of Zn, Ni and O were 69, 7 and 24 wt.%, respectively. Moreover, element mapping of Ni-ZnO (Fig. 4b-f) revealed the uniform distribution of the Ni, Zn and O throughout the Ni-ZnO sample. In addition, the EDX spectrum (Fig. 5 a) and element mapping (Fig. 5b-h) of the N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO sample were carried out to verify the synthesis of the N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO sample. The results confirmed that the N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO nanocomposite was comprised of Zn (63 %), Ni (6%), O (19 %), C (9%), and N (3%). The elemental mapping of the N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO revealed the uniform distribution of the N-CQDs across the surface of the Ni-ZnO nanoparticles.

The FT-IR spectra for the N-CQDs, N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO and ZnO are provided in Fig. 6 . For all samples, a broad absorption band was observed at around 3400 cm−1, which is attributed to OH stretching vibrations. The FT-IR spectrum of the N-CQDs (the red line) showed characteristic absorption peaks at 1575, 1404, 1181 cm−1, which were clearly attributed to the CC, CN and CO stretching vibrations, respectively [32]. THe FT-IR spectrum of the N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO (the green line) revealed three characteristic peaks at 1605, 1384 and 430 cm−1, which were associated with the CC, CN and M–O (Ni-O and Zn-O bonds) stretching vibrations, respectively. In addition, the FT-IR spectrum of ZnO (the blue line) demonstrated an absorption peak at 450 cm−1, possibly corresponding to the Zn-O bonds [33].

As shown in Fig. 7 , the UV–vis spectra of the synthesized nanophotocatalysts proved the superiority of the 1.5 N-CQDs/6wt.% Ni-ZnO over the pure ZnO and 6 wt.% Ni-ZnO for the absorption of visible light. The calculated optical band gaps for these samples are shown in the inset of Fig. 7. On this basis, the 1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO nanophotocatalyst had an optical band gap about 3.025 eV, i.e. lower than the 3.12 and 3.13 eV for the 6 wt.% Ni-ZnO and pure ZnO, respectively. The doping of the Ni ions on the ZnO structure led to the creation of intra-band gap levels, inducing some band gap narrowing [34–36].

The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra serves as a useful tool for investigating the recombination rate and transfer behavior of the photogenerated electrons and holes in a semiconductor. In this respect, the PL spectrum was recorded to study the separation efficiency of the photogenerated charge carriers in the synthesized nanocomposites, as shown in Fig. 8 .

The spectrum of the samples showed a strong emission peak at 390 nm within the UV region and two minor peaks at 420 and 480 nm in the visible regions. The emission peaks at about 420 and 480 nm were possible caused by the interface radiation or surface defects in the ZnO, and the 390 nm peak was probably due to the exciton recombination of ZnO [37–42].

A higher PL emission intensity demonstrates a higher recombination rate of the photogenerated electron/hole pairs. On the other hand, a lower PL emission intensity indicates rapid charge transfer, capturing of electrons at defect sites, and hence lower recombination rate of the photogenerated charge carriers that can improve the photocatalytic activity [37–42].

As can be seen in Fig. 8, for N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO the PL intensity decreased significantly, which could be attributed to effective charge transfer between the Ni-ZnO and N-CQD particles and reducing the recombination process [7,43–46].

According to the literature, the LUMO level of the N-CQDs is in the range of 4.0–4.4 eV, and the conduction band (CB) of pure ZnO is about 2.86 eV, which changes slightly with the addition of nickel [47–51]. Therefore, it is thermodynamically desirable to transfer electrons from the CB of Ni-ZnO to the VB of NCQDs. This implies that the Ni and NCQDs can reduce the recombination rate of the charge carriers and increase the photodegradation efficiency under visible light. This is in agreement with the PL results and MB dye removal experiments (Section 3.1) [7,41,43,48,48,49,50,51].

As mentioned above, ZnO is known as a superior photocatalyst for degradation of organic pollutants. In this work, firstly, ZnO was sensitized to visible light by adding different amounts of nickel ions as dopant. Then, the surface of the Ni-ZnO nanoparticles (with optimum amount of nickel dopant (6 wt.%)) was modified using different amounts of N-CQDs solution. Results of all photocatalytic degradation tests on the MB dye, as a model pollutant, under visible light irradiation using synthesized nanocomposits were explained as follows.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of nickel dopant on photoactivity of ZnO
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 a shows the photocatalytic degradation of MB over Ni-ZnO with different amounts of nickel dopant. As can be seen, the photocatalytic efficiency was improved with increasing the amount of nickel dopant. However, there was an optimum nickel dosage (6 wt.%) beyond which no remarkable effect was observed on the photocatalytic activity of ZnO, possibly due to either of two reasons: (1) the excessive dopant ions induced intrinsic point defects which might serve as recombination centers and act to decrease the photocatalytic activity [52,53], and/or (2) according to XRD results, the excessively dopant-loaded samples (beyond 4 wt.%) showed evidence of the presence of a secondary phase (NiO) that could impose undesirable defects on the surface of the nanophotocatalyst or act as a barrier to light absorption [53–55]. Fig. 9b shows the photocatalytic degradation of MB using 6 wt.% Ni-doped ZnO, leading to a yield of over 64 % after 120 min of illumination under visible light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of N-CQDs on photoactivity of 6wt.% Ni-ZnO
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further sensitize the photocatalyst to the visible light irradiation, the optimum photocatalyst (6 wt.% Ni-ZnO) was modified using different amounts of N-CQDs solution. Fig. 10 displays the photocatalytic degradation of MB by the samples of N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO prepared with different amounts of N-CQDs.

The presence of N-CQDs on the surface of Ni-ZnO particles promoted the separation of the photo-generated charge carriers and improved the MB dye photodegradation. Fig. 10 reveals the photocatalytic activity of the N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO, which was gradually improved with increasing the amounts of attached N-CQDs onto the surface of Ni-ZnO particles. This might be due to the type of bonds, interactions, and/or the effect of small amounts of N-CQDs or its extensive dispersion in the structure, which could enhance the absorbance of the visible light [21,56–58]. However, the photocatalytic activity reduced drastically with increasing the dosage of the N-CQDs beyond the optimal value. This decrease in the light penetration was seemingly responsible for the observed reduction in the photocatalytic activity of the N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO photocatalyst, which might be ascribed to the following reasons: (1) formation of a thicker adhesive layer of carbon dots on the Ni-ZnO surface because of the self-aggregation of N-CQDs (this happens because the N-CQDs tend to collide in the solution and aggregate in the solid state provided their concentration is high during the fabrication of the composites) and (2) reduced surface area upon agglomeration of the N-CQDs on the surface of the photocatalyst and hence fewer sites for activation and absorption of visible light by carbon and graphite [7,45,46,59–66]. The inset of Fig. 10 shows the optimum amount of N-CQDs (1.5 mL) for improving the photoactivity of the 6 wt.% Ni-ZnO by 87 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on photoactivity of 1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic degradation of MB solution over the optimum photocatalyst (1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO) was studied at three pH values of the dye solution.

As shown in Fig. 11 , the optimum condition for degradation of MB over 1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO was an alkaline pH value of about 11 (pH ≈ 11), which could clearly enhance the adsorption of the cationic dye (MB) on the negatively charged surface of the photocatalyst [67]. The negligible photocatalytic activity of the 1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO under acidic condition was attributed to instability of the ZnO photocatalyst in acidic solution [68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of dosage of 1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO nanophotocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the loaded content of the photocatalyst onto the dye solution, degradation experiments were conducted at different dosages of the 1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO into the same MB solution (50 ppm, 50 mL). The obtained results are presented in Fig. 12 . Accordingly, the visible light degradation of MB increased with increasing the amount of 1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO photocatalyst. However, any further increase in the photocatalyst dosage merely added to the turbidity of the MB solution, thereby restricting the contact of light irradiation with the surface of the photocatalyst [32]. As can be seen, 0.05 g of 1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO was appropriate to achieve the highest photocatalytic efficiency for degradation of MB solution under visible light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic study of the MB degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
According to literature, for most organic pollutants (including the MB dye), the kinetics of the photocatalytic degradation under visible light irradiation follows a first-order reaction where the rate constant is calculated by the Langmuir–Hinshelwood (L-H) model, as follows [69,70]:
-ln (C/C0) = kt
where C and C0 are concentrations of the dye solution after and before the photocatalytic reaction, respectively, and k (min−1) and t (min) are reaction rate constant and irradiation time, respectively. The k constant can be estimated from the line slope of the graphing -ln (C/C0) versus irradiation time for each produced nanophotocatalyst.

Fig.13 a and b illustrate the best fit between the experimental data and the l-H model behavior for Ni-ZnO and N-CQD/Ni-ZnO nanocomposites, respectively. Table 2 summarizes the information of the developed lines (k constant and R2) in Fig. 13. The rate constant (k) of the best nanophotocatalyst was found to be 0.015 min−1 with R2 value of 0.909 for 1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt% Ni-ZnO.

Stability and recyclability of the prepared nanophotocatalysts were evaluated by degradation experiments for 6 reaction cycles. After each reaction cycle, the photocatalyst was separated by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 5 min, three-times-washed with DI water and ethanol, and finally dried in an oven at 70 °C overnight.

As can be seen in Fig. 14 , the 1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO photocatalyst exhibited a good stability until 4 successive reaction cycles. However, instability and loss of photoactivity caused upon the separation of N-CQDs from the surface of the Ni-ZnO particles after 4 reaction cycles [7,45,46,59–66,71]. The photocatalytic degradation of MB dropped to as low as 51 % after 6 reaction cycles. Fig. 15 shows the results of the EDX analysis for 1.5 N-CQDs/6 wt.% Ni-ZnO after photodegradation experiments. It is clear that EDX offers an initial estimation of the composition with some levels of error. Nevertheless, the large drop (more than 50 % reduction compared to Fig. 5a) in the carbon and nitrogen contents, indicated a decrease in the content of the N-CQDs on the surface of the nanophotocatalyst due to the particle separation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of photocatalytic degradation using N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO
ce:section.ce:para: After studying the photocatalytic activity of the prepared photocatalyst for the degradation of MB under visible light irradiation, one may suggested that the superior photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO was related to: (i) promoted separation of charges due to the presence of N-CQDs on the surface of Ni-ZnO particles, though the great electron storage capacity of the carbon nanostructures retarded the recombination of the charge carriers, and (ii) sensitization of ZnO photocatalyst for absorbing the visible light by introducing nickel ions into ZnO crystalline structures. In this regards, lower optical band gap of the N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO enhanced the absorption of visible light irradiation upon generating electrons/holes (eˉ/h+). The photo-generated electrons could be transferred to the adsorbed N-CQDs on the surface of the Ni-ZnO particles where they were susceptible to react with the adsorbed oxygen molecules (O2) to generate highly reactive oxygen radicals (•O2ˉ) [72]. In addition, there were also residual holes for reacting with the adsorbed H2O molecules and produce hydroxyl radicals (•OH). These extremely reactive radicals were responsible for mineralization of the dye molecules [73]. This mechanism is schematically shown in Fig. 16 .

Preparation of N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO nanophotocatalyst for degradation of MB solution under visible light irradiation was performed for the first time. The morphology, composition, phase structure, and optical properties of the prepared samples were characterized using SEM and TEM, EDX, XRD, and DRS analyses, respectively. The results showed that the optimized N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO was able to degrade MB at over 87 % under visible light irradiation. In addition, the effect of pH and photocatalyst dosage were investigated for optimizing the photocatalytic activity of the N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO. The kinetics of the MB dye degradation was further modeled according to Langmuir–Hinshelwood model. The excellent photocatalytic activity of the N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO nanocomposite could be attributed to visible light sensitization of ZnO and surface modification of the Ni-ZnO using N-CQDs.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Reza Behnood: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Software. Gholamhossein Sodeifian: Conceptualization, Visualization, Investigation, Supervision, Software, Validation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank research deputy of University of Kashan for providing financial support for this project (Grant # Pajoohaneh-1398/16). Moreover, the authors express their thanks to the advanced materials and nanotechnology laboratory of University of Kashan for providing experimental facilities.
